display_name: AAA authentication failure — timeout means server unreachable, reject means bad credentials, but logs say the same thing
context: Syslog or AAA debug output showing authentication failures for user logins, 802.1X, or device administration
observation: Logs show authentication failed for a user or session; the AAA server (RADIUS or TACACS+) appears to be rejecting credentials
naive_interpretation: The credentials are wrong or the user account is locked/expired on the AAA server — investigate the account configuration on the server
correct_interpretation: AAA authentication failures have two fundamentally different causes that produce similar syslog messages: (1) TIMEOUT — the AAA server never responded, meaning the server is down, unreachable, or a firewall is blocking the RADIUS/TACACS+ port; (2) REJECT — the server responded with Access-Reject, meaning the credentials, authorization policy, or account state is wrong. The fix for a timeout is network/infrastructure (reachability, firewall rules, server health); the fix for a reject is credential/policy configuration on the server. Most NOS platforms distinguish these in debug output or detailed AAA counters, but the high-level syslog message often just says 'authentication failed' for both. Always check whether the server responded at all before investigating credentials.
why: RADIUS uses UDP — a timeout produces no error response, just silence; the NAS (network access server) logs this as a failure after the retry timer expires
TACACS+ uses TCP — a connection refused or timeout is distinguishable from a successful TCP connection that returns a FAIL status, but syslog may collapse both into 'authentication failed'

When multiple AAA servers are configured, a timeout on the primary causes failover to the secondary; if both timeout, the device may fall back to local auth — this can look like intermittent 'wrong password' when the real problem is server reachability
NOS AAA counters (requests sent, timeouts, rejects) separate the two failure modes definitively; syslog alone does not

confirmation_commands.purpose: Compare access-requests sent vs timeouts vs access-rejects to determine if the server is responding at all
Check TACACS+ request/response counters to distinguish timeout from explicit reject
Display per-server transaction counts including timeouts, failures, and successful authentications

confirmation_commands.command: test aaa group radius server <ip> <username> <password> legacy
confirmation_commands.purpose: Test RADIUS authentication to a specific server to see if it responds or times out
Check RADIUS server response statistics to identify timeouts vs actual rejections

traps: Reading 'authentication failed' in syslog and immediately investigating credentials on the AAA server without checking if the server even responded
Not checking AAA server statistics (timeouts vs rejects) which definitively distinguish the two failure modes
Confusing RADIUS timeout behavior (silent UDP drop) with an explicit rejection — RADIUS timeouts produce no response packet at all
Overlooking firewall rules blocking UDP 1812/1813 (RADIUS) or TCP 49 (TACACS+) after a network change, which causes timeouts that look like auth failures
Assuming local auth fallback means the AAA server is working — it may mean all remote servers timed out and the device fell through to local

sources.title: RFC 2865 — Remote Authentication Dial In User Service (RADIUS)
